speaker: interviewer
text: By Leah Shifrin Averick on December 17, 1996 in Des Plaines, Illinois.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that we were-- a train-- on a train from Berlin to Zerbst, which was our--

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd left--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we left Berlin. Yeah, we were, and still, everything was fairly normal. I mean, it was a regular train, a special train, that took us to Zerbst.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you weren't afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet, no. We were not afraid because nothing seemed to be sinister and threatening at-- at-- at this-- at that-- at that very time, I mean, at the-- at the travel. And it was only a short trip-- it was like maybe a three-hour trip on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did You know anybody on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: But these were all Mischlings?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we're all the same type of person, like I'm-- like myself. So we went there--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you ask them, why-- why are we here? And they said, you're a [NON-ENGLISH], you know? You are-- that's-- so everybody-- everybody-- I mean was exchanging stories from where they had been coming from, and so forth-- where they had lived before. They all said, we are here for the same reason, because we're half-Jewish. So they're sending us to this-- they're sending us to a camp. We don't know anything about this camp, yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we go, and we-- we arrived there about three hours later. And that's when the seriousness-- when the real facts became-- we became aware of what's going-- what our life is going to be now, that we're going to be incarcerated. And it's going to be very, very, you know, strict, and also probably dangerous. Because big trucks, army trucks, were standing ready for us, to transport us.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the-- the-- the railroad only took us to Zerbst, the city of Zerbst. And then the camp was maybe-- it was actually the airfield, the German air force-- Luftwaffe airfield, that was maybe eight or 10 miles from the city-- maybe less. And so the trucks-- we had to board the trucks, and-- Something already happened back then. Somebody-- we were crowded, they didn't have enough trucks, and they had trailers attached-- one trailer to each truck. And these were all pickup-- like open, they were open. And we were standing up. And they didn't have enough, so really enough trucks, so we were kind of crowded. And somebody fell out of the truck, out of the front part of the truck, and fell right in front of the wheels of the trailer-- because the trailer was, of course, were attached. And the trailer went over. And I think he was either seriously hurt or killed-- I don't really remember, because they took him away. Yeah, and there was-- just at the beginning, there was an accident, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: That they-- that they didn't care about this person. Is that the attitude?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- I'm not so sure-- I'm not so sure that they didn't care, but I don't think that the driver noticed. These were army drivers-- SS drivers. I don't think they noticed it until they noticed that the trailer bumped or something, you know? I mean, you don't see this kind of truck too often. Now you see them here, that they have two and three trailers. But this was just a regular truck with a pickup body. And then you had another-- just a trailer-- with a pickup body that he was pulling. So that was not very good. That put a really bad damper on the-- on this going there. And then, when we got to the camp, we were-- realized that this is an airport-- German airfield, air force airfield. And there, we were greeted by these people from the paramilitary group of Organization Todt, T-O-D-T, which was a construction-- comparable to the American Seabees during World War II. They were part of the Navy, but these were-- special, different. They worked with all of the military branches whenever construction work had to be done. And so they were in charge of us. They were-- and they were-- they headed the day-to-day operation of the camp. So we were all put into-- assigned to barracks, which had previously been used by air force, German air force personnel-- in which were in no way sufficient for the 500 people. And so that meant that, you know, maybe five, six bunks to-- per room. And that meant that some of the people had to sleep on the floor, a concrete floor, on straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started-- so then, the next day-- When you say greeted, what do you mean by greeted? They welcomed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- they received us and said, you are-- you are here, and you're going to have to-- you know, you're going to be working on-- on-- on this-- on constructing the air-- taxiways and tarmacs for this airport. Because before, you didn't need any taxi and concrete air runways. You could land on just a flat grass. But they-- these were jets that they were using, and it was the first operational jet fighter plane. It was a twin engine jet fighter, bomber, that was called Messerschmidt 500-- 252, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they explain all this to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- we just saw them. They just said, there are planes here, jet planes. These are new planes. They need the-- they need runways. And you're going to be building those runways-- that's your job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started doing this, all right, the next day. We were out there early. What were your hours? When did you get up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to get up, I think, at 6 o'clock they came and woke us up. We had work clothes. They gave us work clothes, like overalls. We did not have the uniforms. That's another distinction from concentration camps. We did not have those striped outfits. We had regular work clothes-- might have been former air force overalls-- work-- work clothing from the air force that they had left and that they gave us. And then we started working every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. No tattoos on your arms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no-- No, that's another distinction. No, that was only in the regular concentration camps that you had-- So they did-- You know, we got up every morning-- There were a number of problems. First of all, they wouldn't give us enough food. I mean, the food was very limited. You know, there was a mess hall where we would all march and get our rations. And it was never enough. I mean, it was very-- we did not get-- we didn't share the food with the air force personnel, German air force personnel. They had a special kitchen for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They made it pretty clear that, you know-- that-- that we are expected to do the work, and even if it's hard work, and not to complain about it, and do it-- because otherwise you're going to be sent to possibly to a more severe camp, you know? And you knew about these more severe camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we knew about the concentration camps. That talk had become-- you know-- it was-- had become-- still, it was not-- wide-- I mean, it was not real widely known. But there was a level of knowledge among people that nobody dared to really to talk about it. But everybody was whispering it. But people did know about the concentration camp, had heard. Somebody knew somebody who had-- maybe even a guard there or something. People had-- knew about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they know? What did you know about it or hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all we knew is that those camps were, you know-- pretty-- pretty sinister. They were pretty-- you know, where people would probably be tortured and killed. We did not really know about the mass-- I mean, nobody really realized at the time, in '43 and '44, at the mass exterminations of Jewish people-- of the burning and the gassing them, and then burning the ashes in those big ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, they did-- no, that was not really-- but they knew the terrible-- people had some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] idea that there were terrible things going on. They just didn't know all the details. You know? You knew they didn't come back? Did you know they wouldn't come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because nobody expected them back. They were incarcerated. As long as the Nazi Germany is in charge of-- you know, as long as they're in power, they wouldn't be coming back. So, you know, nobody was questioning why-- if they-- why they haven't come back yet. Because they knew as long as this state, this regime is in power, there will nothing change.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was an-- hanging over our heads, this threat that, if you don't do the work, you know-- but it was-- If you don't do the work, then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you don't do the work, you may be-- you may be sent to a place worse than this. So this camp wasn't that worse-- wasn't that bad, I mean. It wasn't-- there weren't that many-- you know-- we were-- we were guarded, but there was-- there was no wall or barbed wire around our compound.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were guarded by those guys. They-- they kept an eye on us. The foreman of this organization taught. They were-- they were-- they were the people that assigned us the work, and also, they kept an eye on us. But if one wanted to escape, they could've escaped. In fact, I didn't escape, but this is something that I didn't even mention in my article that I wrote about this. One day I went out-- because we had so little food. They give us such limited portions, and you're doing heavy, physical work. you know, I'm 18 years old. I have an appetite like a bear. So it was never enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I noticed that some of my other buddies in the camp, they went outside. And they raided some of the silos put-- that held potatoes of surrounding farms. Highly different-- I did it one day, myself. I went up in the middle of the night or early morning. You just walked out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked out. They were not, yeah, because they were not guarding, but it was senseless to try to get away because you had no clothes on. Even wore what you got-- and no identification paper. I mean, you would-- you would immediately-- somewhere-- even if you could get away from there, a good distance from the camp. But-- I walked-- I got up one morning with a sack, with a small bag, or-- and I went out, and I went to one of those silos. And I got-- really was scared, because I could hear the guard-- the German shepherds, the dogs, barking in the front. They did have dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the guards had dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, but they didn't catch on to that. The guards were very haphazard, very, you know, laid back. The only thing that you could get in trouble with them, if you didn't do the work. You know? And the guards were all very-- I wouldn't say low class, but they were not a very intelligent group of people. They were all-- I'm not trying to, you know, to make a-- a-- you know-- a discouraging statement about this department-- you know, downgrading construction people-- construction workers. But they were no match, really, for people like us. People that were in the camp, ranging from lawyers-- and even the German army officer who was in there, too, who had been recalled from the front and sent to the camp, because suddenly they decided that no longer can half Aryans be in the German army. But they were up until that point. And this guy was even, I think, he was a lieutenant. There were all kinds of people-- doctors-- you know, all kinds of highly professional people. They were no match for people that only built houses and break another building. The other In charge-- that's what they are, construction people.

speaker: interviewer
text: With dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: With dogs, yeah, they had dogs. So I went out and got potatoes one morning. And I got back, and I-- I hid them in-- in my-- under my bunk. And every night when I got-- somehow I got a pot, and I think-- and somehow I was able to make a fire and cook myself some potatoes. Just eat-- that was supplementing the meager diet that we're getting in the normal-- that we get normally, with some potatoes. You know? That was a welcome addition, because we were so hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You built the fire inside your bunk? Outside? Where did you build the fire?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think they had a little stove there-- somebody had a stove in one of the other rooms, because that was left over when the air force people had that. And everybody was using that stove. There was a common place to use it-- a lot of the guys. But it was dangerous. I had-- I was pretty scared when I did this, when I was up out of the compound, you know, and heard those dogs barking. I mean, the guards knew, sometimes, I think. I had the feeling that they knew we were doing this, but they didn't interfere with it. As long as we were coming back, and as long as the number-- every day when then roll call came, we were still at the same number.

words.text: they had a little stove there--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a roll call every morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: For a long wait, a short wait? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was short-- it was a-- because they didn't want to waste any time. They needed to-- to maybe get on with the work. You see, and everybody-- we all got the-- the camp really was under the-- those-- those construction people, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] paramilitary people. They were only there to lead us, to instruct us, and to supervise us for the work. The camp really was under the authority of the SS. And like-- and then every day, an SS major came and drove into the camp and talked to us. Actually, we all talked to him every day. But he was driven in an SS staff car by an SS private.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was so-- he was taunting, us every day. And he was an intelligent man. We found out later that he was, in civilian life, he was a lawyer. You know? But he was an SS-- he was in charge of our camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was taunting us all the time. He was telling us, yeah, you guys are OK. You're doing-- but it doesn't matter how good a job you do, because you're not going to live long to enjoy all these-- all these things. You're not-- your days are counted. Everyday he'd come to us, and we would just look at him. There was no sense in getting into an argument with him, because anybody who would get in an argument, he'd be hauled off like that and have to go with them. So we didn't say anything, but he came every day and did the same spiel over and over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're saying it won't--

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't-- it doesn't count. It doesn't matter how good you are. And if you do all your work, down to the-- to the letter, in the end, you're going to be executed regardless. Because that's what the long range plans are for people like you. Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- that's a-- a very interesting. That gives you a real good [INAUDIBLE]. So you think that made us, working like dogs, and then becoming, really like eager to do all this work? No, you resist this. Every one of those 500 people was not interested in seeing the war prolonged and seeing this airfield, the Luftwaffe airfield intact, and seeing that those fighters can take off and shooting down allied planes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every one of those steps would only be against us, would prolong the war. And that would possibly put us at risks that we'd never--never survive. So it was-- nobody really said anything about it. But it became a routine of work every day that people appeared to be either dumb, acting dumb, or clumsy. But there was an organized-- a very orchestrated effort to undermine the-- the work and the building of that airfield, and doing everything with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me some examples of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, I remember one day an army truck came, and they had a giant roll with those telephone cables on there, like you see on those extra large-- I don't know what you call these-- wheels, cables. We were as supposed to string it out, take it off the truck, and string it. And it was pretty heavy wire from one of the hangars to another one. And suddenly, they ordered us to take it off, and they commanded about-- take 20, 30, 40 guys. I don't know how heavy it was. The guards said take it off. It became a tug of war. It took-- 25 guys pulled on that side, and 25 or 30 guys on the other side. Before you know, the dumb thing split. I can't believe how it happened, but it split. Well, they couldn't replace things like that. This was another you know-- another-- another incident where it was impossible that would-- hurting the war effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was purposefully, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. It was done purposely. But nobody talked about it. There was just quietly- that was passive-- what you call passive resistance. Active resistance we couldn't do. They would shoot. They would-- they would take, certainly, stern measures, I mean, if we openly showed our defiance. But this-- they thought we were stupid. You know-- honestly, I cannot believe. One day I was ordered to take cement sacks, because for the concrete mixer off of the truck. When the guard goes up-- one of these Organization Todt people goes up-- and he says-- he has a line of us standing up. Everybody is supposed to throw down a sack.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't how heavy it was, maybe 100 pounds, maybe heavier. Now do I look like the guy that you can throw from a truck? Throw and expect me to catch it? I dropped the darn thing. Purposely?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know what-- purposely. And you know what happens when you drop a-- a-- a bag of cement? It just splits open, wide open and into dust. So then another one, another one of those was ruined. They didn't say anything. They just cursed me for being an idiot and clumsy-- which I wasn't. It was all done purposefully, everyone--

speaker: interviewer
text: Consciously.

speaker: interviewee
text: Consciously. It was done with a very deep purpose to-- because we all knew. This guy comes over, tells us every day, you're going to be executed. It doesn't matter what-- how good you are, or how bad you are. You're going to be executed anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a timetable for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is what he said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a timetable. As it turned  out, at the Nuremberg war crimes trials-- the later one, you know, after the end of war-- the timetable was revealed. It was 1946 that they were planning to take those same measures that against the regular Jews, they would take against us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Mischlings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was in the-- was in the final solution. There was the timetable. So fortunately, the war ended in May of 1945. So-- that's-- you know-- but this went on and on every day. One day, I know that I got in an argument with one of the officers, Organization Todt superiors that was in charge of our detail. I don't know what happened, but he struck me. And I don't know if I had said something that he didn't like. I can't remember, but in any case, he-- he-- he took-- took my-- I dropped my glasses. He brushed them off, you know, when he struck me. He didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: He struck you where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he struck me in the face on the cheek on the side. You know-- and he knocked off my glasses, and they broke. And that was a bad situation because I'm so nearsighted. So he gave me-- he came up with a-- I said, look, I can't work. You know-- And he had-- they had-- excuse me, they had-- were prepared for everything. They had extra glasses and lenses, and he pulled out his office desk drawer, and he gave me another pair of glasses, honestly. Otherwise, I couldn't have been seeing. But somebody had gotten in real trouble. He must have said something, either insolent-- one of these-- you know, he couldn't-- I mean, they were not smart those guys. But you shouldn't-- it was unwise to insult them outright. I mean, that was dumb. You know-- you you do that. You better bite your tongue.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they-- when they did what? Bite your tongue at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at some of the orders that they gave us, or just making remarks about them, how stupid they are. I mean, that was not a good idea because if they catch you and hear you, then they can angry or really mad.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were taunting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were making comments about how dumb they are. You know-- and we have to be-- we have to follow them. We have to take their orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but one of the guys, I think, was-- got some-- got-- was hit. I don't know if he was hit with a belt or with a whip-- probably with a belt. And he complained that he had hurt his kidneys and so forth. But that's the only thing. Brutality was not a-- Prevalent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Overriding factor, or a real prevalent factor. But they didn't treat us with-- with gloves, you know. Well, with gloves, I mean. They were rough. These people are, by nature, rough. And if you ever watch construction workers, I mean, they are-- you know. But I'm not trying to downgrade them. It was just our unfortunate situation that we had to work with them. But probably in a way, it was probably much better than being guarded by the regular SS guards like the concentration camps had. Because these people were only interested-- we became, actually, friendly with the pilots. I could not believe that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the pilots figure in? Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew about us. They knew why we were there, that we were inmates. They were actually sympathetic to us. They liked us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the pilots come? After the runways were built?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after the runways. While we were building them, they started using them. We would go up. Nobody stopped us. We would go up. They would come climb out of their Messerschmitt jets, and they would talk. We would talk to them. In fact, we would do business with them. Some reason, I don't know how they did it, but some of our people in the camp got cigarettes and chocolates, candy smuggled in. I don't know how they did it, and they would exchange them, trade them with the pilots. They wanted-- they-- didn't-- evidently the Germany air force didn't give them enough cigarette and candy rations. They would take ours, and they gave us canned foods and some of their food items. Yeah, that was-- they knew about us, and they-- they felt sympathetic. In fact, I think if we had involved them in-- in-- in conversations, they-- they might have even said, listen, we-- we've-- we feel with you guys, you know. We think maybe the war is not going to be won. You know-- and so they treated us like normal human beings. They had no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: But see, no-- don't forget. These were regular soldiers. You know? There was a difference. Who was a regular soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: These air force these pilots. They were Luftwaffe soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were officers, weren't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them, yeah. But they were not-- what I'm saying, they were not hardened Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: None of them. They didn't have to be members of the Nazi party. They were chosen because of their leadership qualities and because of their expertise in flying airplanes. In this case, these first operational jets of the whole 20th century. Nobody had them. It was quite an advance.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end of the war. This is 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was 1944, 1945, toward the end the war. That's why I couldn't understand when-- we always thought, how can they fly, how can they fight enemy planes that are propeller driven, because they are so much slower. These jets were flying 400, 500 miles like the jets today. And how much do the regular B-17s and the Mustang planes-- they were all propeller planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every time the air raid sirens would go on, all those guys scrambled into their planes and took off and flew east instead of west. Instead of leading the oncoming-- I couldn't understand that, but I found later through William Shirer's book about D-Day, and so forth, he has-- they did-- they did use some of those jets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And apparently, they were quite successful even though they are so much faster. Because by the time they have zeroed in on a target-- propelled plane-- they had already passed it. But they apparently downed some of the Allied planes, even with those jets. But normally, they just took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they fly east?

speaker: interviewee
text: To keep them from being destroyed if the airport is attacked, which happened. That satisfaction we got before it all ended. You know-- at the-- toward the-- it was like in early April, they were getting a-- every day there were air raids. And every day we would interrupt our work and seek shelter, duck into trenches.

speaker: interviewer
text: And looking up to the sky, we see those. And those planes in World War II, they were only flying at 10,000 feet or less than that because that was the height. See those B-17s and then the fighter planes. And every day, every time this happened, we were wondering when will it be? Because we knew, and American Allied intelligence knew about this airport. When will it be? They waited for the final day when everything was finished of our work. That's when they waited for. Every day they passed us and attacked Magdeburg, and Erfurt, and Schweinfurt, and all those cities. We were praying. We were practically praying, when are they going to come for this airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day-- it was a terrible day. They actually-- it's lucky, I don't think we lost anybody, but we could have lost a lot of people. One day, I see those bomber formations approaching, and the lead plane is dropping these smoke-- smoke markers from the-- that I know. I was with a little kid, that I scrapped in my article. He was younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name? What was your little kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to say Joachim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Joachim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was with him in the-- we just happened to be on the work detail together. And when this all started, it was in the daylight, broad daylight. And I see. I said to him, Joachim, this is us, because at the beginning, at one end of the airfield, they're dropping those-- the bombers are dropping those markers. But the jets had all taken off prior to that, so there were no jets left. They had all taken off. So we were laying in a-- in a crater that was created by-- from a previous attack but that was just an isolated bomb. We were ducking into that, cowering in there, and all hell broke loose. It was impossible. They just rained the bombs down. It was so bad. I mean, all of it was dust clouds, and there were forests around us. And it was just-- I don't know how we lived through it. It was total destruction. Bombs were raining all around us, but nobody was-- miraculously, nobody was killed from our camp people. They were all in-- in-- in-- in safety and shelters or in the trenches. It was just, you know-- an-- an incredible day. But it gave us the greatest satisfaction. We had waited for that day, prayed for that day to come to see this destroyed, because that would mean another setback for the German military. And this is exactly what happened. You know-- I could not-- I-- I never forget this day. But it wasn't-- there where a lot of close calls. When we finally crawled out of that crater-- but there was, like I said, an old bomb crater.

speaker: interviewer
text: An old bomb crater--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from the previous war, just an isolated bomb. And it was impossible. You couldn't see anything. The whole airfield, everything was one giant cloud of dust, and we thought everything is destroyed. So we just looked at us, and I think we were all--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with Joachim, your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we all thought this is the day we have waited for. They finally did it. You know? So I-- there are some others. One day, actually, during one of the air raids when the airport was not attacked, they-- they shot down a-- yeah, they shot down one of the accompanying Mustang fighters that accompanied the bomber periods. So-- we actually saw the pilot, and turned out he was black. And the Germans could not believe that they captured-- he surrendered to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
